coredata.dc:title: Controllable excitation-dependent fluorescence triggered by the increasing graphitic nitrogen in carbon dots and its application in multi-analyte detection
coredata.dc:creator: Ye, Changqing
Xu, Lei
Chen, Shuoran
Wang, Caihong
Su, Meng
Dai, Guoliang
Wang, Xiaomei
Li, Fengyu
Song, Yanlin
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) with controllable excitation-dependent fluorescence (EDFL) were elaborately prepared for the application of multiplex metal ion discrimination. A serial of CD samples were synthesized through a simple solvothermal reaction in which extra nitrogen sources with enhancing chemical reducibility were added into a specific nitrogen-contained precursor. Both experimental characterizations and theoretical calculations based on density functional theory have proved that the functional group of graphitic nitrogen (GN) is the key factor to achieve EDFL, and the content of GN was determined by the reducibility strength of extra nitrogen sources in the synthesis. In that case, sensing channels with different response behaviours to multiplex analytes can be formed due to the controllable EDFL of CDs. As a result, seventeen types of metal ions can be detected and recognized through the as-prepared CD probes. This work provides a novel angle to understand and engineer the EDFL property of CDs, as well as a facile strategy to appropriately construct high effective sensing channels for multi-analyte detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Graphitic nitrogen
Excitation-dependent fluorescence
Multi-analyte detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Theoretical calculation for simulation of the optical properties of the CDs
Multiplex metal-ion detection based on the CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the synthetic strategy of CD1 - 4. (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of CD1 - 4.
FL spectra of CD1 - 4 under different excitation wavelengths from 265 to 440 nm. The insets: photographs of the FL beams.
Structure characterization of CDs. (a–d) XPS survey of CD1 - 4; (e–h) N1S XPS spectra of CD1 - 4.

DFT calculation for simulation of the optical properties of the CDs. (a) Calculated UV–vis absorption spectra for the nitrogen-doped models (N1, GN1, GN2) and undoped model N0; (b) Differences between the HOMO and LUMO (H−L gaps) clearly indicate narrowing of the H−L gap in the GN doped models. (c) Calculated FL spectra of the emission maxima of the N-doped systems N1 (red), GN1 (blue), and GN2 (green), as well as undoped model N0 (black), demonstrating EDFL only in the GN-doped models. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Multiplex metal-ion detection results. a) 3D representation of PCA result shows a clear clustering of the seventeen types of metal ions. b) HCA analysis gives the similarity clustering of the seventeen types of metal ions.
Schematic illustration of the increasing content of the GN in carbon dots can trigger EDFL property, which can be applied in multi-analyte detection.

head.ce:title: Controllable excitation-dependent fluorescence triggered by the increasing graphitic nitrogen in carbon dots and its application in multi-analyte detection

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory for Flexible Photonics and Electronics Sensing
College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Laboratory for Flexible Photonics and Electronics Sensing, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Jinan University, Guangzhou, 510632, P.R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) with controllable excitation-dependent fluorescence (EDFL) were elaborately prepared for the application of multiplex metal ion discrimination. A serial of CD samples were synthesized through a simple solvothermal reaction in which extra nitrogen sources with enhancing chemical reducibility were added into a specific nitrogen-contained precursor. Both experimental characterizations and theoretical calculations based on density functional theory have proved that the functional group of graphitic nitrogen (GN) is the key factor to achieve EDFL, and the content of GN was determined by the reducibility strength of extra nitrogen sources in the synthesis. In that case, sensing channels with different response behaviours to multiplex analytes can be formed due to the controllable EDFL of CDs. As a result, seventeen types of metal ions can be detected and recognized through the as-prepared CD probes. This work provides a novel angle to understand and engineer the EDFL property of CDs, as well as a facile strategy to appropriately construct high effective sensing channels for multi-analyte detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted significant research attention due to the superior optical properties since the fluorescence (FL) property of CDs was firstly reported in 2004 [1]. CDs have revealed many advantageous properties such as water-solubility, chemical-stability, non-toxicity and biocompatibility [2–4], making them promising candidates for applications in lighting [5], sensing [6–8], imaging [9,10], catalysis [11–13], display [14], and photovoltaics [15–17]. Many synthetic strategies have been reported to tune the optical properties of CDs from blue to red emission. Among them, most reflect the typical band-gap tuning by size modulation [18]. Also, doping heteroatoms such as nitrogen, boron or sulfur is another simple and effective way to control the FL performance of CDs [19–21]. Due to the complex composition and structure of these doped CDs [22], understanding the origin of the FL may enlighten the rational design of CD optical properties [23,24]. For example, nitrogen can be embedded in CDs in the forms of various functional groups like amino, amidic, pyridinic, pyrrolic, graphitic or nitro nitrogen [25,26]. It has been demonstrated that amino or hydrazine surface groups on CDs can cause a wavelength shift from blue to green regions [27,28]. Nevertheless, the influence of nitrogen doping on the FL properties of the CDs has not been fully understood, particularly about its control toward the excitation-dependent fluorescence emission (EDFL) [29,30], which is the most unique feature among those superior optical properties of CDs. Even worse, in many reports the red shifting of the FL wavelength in CDs was often accompanied by strong decrease in FL intensity [31]. Therefore, further understanding and rationally engineering the EDFL of CDs are very desirable at the current time. Based on this uniquely spectroscopic characteristic of EDFL, CD-based sensors can be perfectly employed in multi-analyte detection due to the astonishing response behaviours of EDFL to different analytes [31–33]. The design and control of EDFL could be the key factor to easily achieve multi-dimensional sensing channels [34].

Herein, we are seeking a means to achieve the synthesis of CDs with controllable EDFL. By using precursors with different chemical reducibility in solvothermal reactions, a facile strategy to synthesize CDs doped with controllable amount of the graphitic nitrogen (GN) was developed. Theoretical and experimental relations between certain structures and EDFL were demonstrated. A clear insight into the FL emission variation during the increase of GN is of vital importance to understand the EDFL. Briefly, it was explored that when no extra nitrogen source or weak reducibility extra nitrogen source was involved in the synthesis, no GN can be found in the samples, and the CDs only exhibited single FL emission. Only when the reducibility of the extra nitrogen source is strong enough, GN can emerge in CD samples, yielding additional red-shifting FL emissions at longer excitation wavelength, i.e. EDFL. Furthermore, as the reducibility of extra nitrogen source gets stronger, the resulting content of GN in CDs increases. Since the single FL and EDFL emission signals from the CD samples have shown totally diverse response behaviours to different types of metal ions, they can be effectively employed in multiplex metal ion discrimination (Scheme 1 ). The optimum result demonstrates that seventeen types of metal ions can be detected and recognized by probes based on the as-prepared CDs. All these results will provide a new angle to understand and engineer the EDFL property of CDs, which will contribute to CDs sensing applications in many areas, particularly in multi-analyte detection.

5-aminoisophthalic acid (5-AIPA), isophthalic acid, lead chloride were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Reagent Company. Ethylenediamine (EDA), ammonia, hydrazine hydrate, ethanolamine and all the other metal salts were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All reagents were of analytical grade and used as received. Twice-distilled water was used throughout all the experiments.

All CDs were synthesized through solvothermal reactions. 0.018 g (0.1 mmol) 5-AIPA were dissolved in 10 mL ethanol. Then, extra nitrogen sources (78 μL aqua ammonia for CD2, 67 μL EDA for CD3 and 49 μL hydrazine hydrate for CD4) were added to the mixture under vigorous stirring to form a homogeneous solution. Next, the mixture was placed into a 50 mL Teflonlined autoclave, heated at 180 °C for 10 h, and then cooled to room temperature in air. The as-prepared CDs were purified by dialysis tube (1000 Da, molecular weight cutoff) for 3 days. The CD solution was then concentrated using a rotary evaporator to 10 mL and diluted with water before measurement.

Typically, 2 mL of metal ion (1.0 mM) solution was added into 2 mL of CD solution (0.01 mg mL−1), and then the mixture was kept static to react for 20 min at room temperature before the FL spectral measurements. Finally, the associated FL spectra were recorded. All seventeen types of metal ions were measured under the same condition.

UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a UV2600 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). FL emission spectra were recorded on a FLS920 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were recorded on a Nicolet 670 FT-IR spectrometer in the form of KBr pellets. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images were taken on a JEOL-2100F transmission electron microscope. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were recorded on a Themo SCIENTIFIC ESCALAB 250 spectrometer with Al Kα X-ray radiation (1486.6 eV).

Our facile strategy to prepare CDs with controllable amount of GN for multi-analyte detection is illuminated in Fig. 1 a. Briefly, four types of CDs (named as CD1-4) are mainly investigated and discussed in this work, which were all synthesized through solvothermal reactions. The precursor is a common organic synthetic intermediate, 5-AIPA, which contains an amino group in order to ensure a basic nitrogen doping amount. The extra nitrogen sources, which are ammonia (CD2), EDA (CD3) and hydrazine hydrate (CD4), are based on amine compounds with enhancing chemical reducibility. Normalized UV–vis absorption spectra of CD1 - 4 are shown in Fig. 1b. All CD samples have a strong absorption peak at ca. 320 nm, and another absorption peak at a longer wavelength of ca. 400 nm emerges in CD3 and CD4. Moreover, by adding extra nitrogen source with stronger reducibility, CD4 shows higher absorption intensity at the longer wavelength area.

The appearance of the second absorption peak in Fig. 1b suggests dual FL emissions of the CDs, which are verified by FL spectra shown in Fig. 2 . The FL spectra of each CD sample are recorded under different excitation wavelengths (265–440 nm, per 25 nm), respectively. CD1 and CD2 show a single FL emission at ca. 400 nm (Fig. 2a and b), meanwhile the EDFL property, represented by dual FL emissions (ca. 420 and 510 nm), are shown in CD3 and CD4 (Fig. 2c and d). The long wavelength emission at ca. 510 nm can be achieved by excitation from 365 to 415 nm, which matches the absorption result in Fig. 1b. The photographs of the FL beams (blue in CD1 and CD2; blue and green in CD3 and CD4) are shown in the inset. All CD samples for the optical measurements were dispersed in water, and the solution exhibits high transparency under daylight and bright FL under irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. S1). Moreover, in order to gather a clearer insight into the FL emission variation with different reducibility of the extra nitrogen sources in our synthetic strategy, two more types of CDs are also prepared. A different precursor with similar structure to 5-AIPA by only missing an amino group, isophthalic acid, was used to synthesize a reference sample (CD0). By comparing the optical performance between CD0 and CD1, nitrogen contained precursor 5-AIPA can significantly enhance the FL intensity of the corresponding CDs (Fig. S2). Moreover, another amine extra nitrogen source, ethanolamine, was also employed, and the corresponding CD sample (CD5) reveals optical performance similar to CD2 due to the weak reducibility nature of ethanolamine (Fig. S3). Besides, the influence of the extra nitrogen source amount added in the synthesis was also investigated (Fig. S13), which showed that the green FL emission got stronger as the concentration of hydrazine hydrate increased, suggesting an increasing of the doped GN content. To verify the different features among the FL emissions obtained from all CD samples, fluorescence excitation (FLE) spectra (Fig. S4) and FL lifetime (Fig. S5 and Table S1) were also measured. FLE spectra of the dual FL emissions of CD3 and CD4 show great difference from the single FL emission of CD1 and CD2, indicating different FL states before and after doping with strong reducibility extra nitrogen source, which can also be confirmed by different FL lifetimes of all CD samples, representing different fluorescence states [35].

Structure characterizations were performed to investigate the connection between structure and property of the CDs. Firstly, the size and morphology of CDs are characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. S6, CDs exhibit quasi-spherical shape with average size of 3–5 nm.

High-resolution TEM observation of CD3 and CD4 in the insets exhibits lattice spacing of 0.22 nm, indicating lattice plane (100) of graphitic carbon, which is the foundation of GN before doping. Moreover, similar phenomena could not be found in the HRTEM observation of CD1 and CD2, indicating amorphous structures. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) results reveal that the doped nitrogen content increases from CD1 (8.0%) to CD4 (19.9%) (Fig. 3 a–d and Table S2), although the nitrogen amount employed in all the synthetic reactions was kept the same. The increasing trend of the doped nitrogen content was in perfect accordance with the FL quantum yield (Table S7). The enhancing reducibility of extra nitrogen sources resulted in higher chemical reactivity, leading to the higher nitrogen doping efficiency in preperation, and stronger reducibility contributes to forming graphitic structure [36]. Fig. 3e–h and Table S2 also reveal high-resolution N1S spectra of CD1 - 4. The relative contents of the four types of nitrogen-contained doped structures (pyrrolic nitrogen, pyridinic nitrogen, amino nitrogen and GN) are measured, showing that only when strong reducibility extra nitrogen sources were involved in the synthesis, GN can be found in the corresponding CDs and its relative content increased as the reducibility of nitrogen source got stronger (5.3% in CD3 and 25.6% in CD4, highlighted by the purple area). This speculation can also be confirmed by FT-IR measurement (Fig. S8) [37]. IR spectra of CD3 and CD4 display more significant peaks at 1360 and 1650 cm−1, which are ascribed to C–N= and CN bonds, demonstrating an increasing content of GN [38–40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Theoretical calculation for simulation of the optical properties of the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: For further understanding of the influence of nitrogen doping on the optical properties of CDs, computationally tractable models of chromophores containing nitrogen atoms were evaluated by time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT). All the models are pyrene derivatives, which have been well-applied in previous studies [21,26]. Because all the models are small molecules, there is no doubt that their optical properties are not completely the same comparing to the CD samples. However, the relative evolution trend of the optical performance from CD1 to CD4 can be simulated by the computed results, suggesting the relations between GN and EDFL. The four pyrene-based models (N0, N1, GN1, GN2) containing carboxyl, hydroxyl and nitrogen atoms are shown in Fig. 4 a. The structures of the four models are designed to simulate the structure evolution trend from CD1 to CD4 according to the experimental data. Model N0 is a reference model, representing samples without nitrogen doping (CD0). Model N1 contains a pyridinic N, representing samples doped by nitrogen but no GN (CD1 and CD2). Model GN1 and GN2 contain one and two GN, representing CD samples of relative less (CD3) and more (CD4) GN, respectively. The calculated absorption spectra are shown in Fig. 4a and Table S4. The narrowing of HOMO-LUMO (H-L) gaps of model GN1 and GN2 shown in Fig. 4b indicate that the presence of GN suggests FL emission at longer wavelength. Calculated FL spectra in Fig. 4c and Table S5 prove this speculation by revealing single FL emission in N0 and N1, and dual FL emissions in GN1 and GN2. Moreover, the FL emission at longer wavelength in GN2 displays higher relative intensity than GN1, indicating that more doped GNs provide stronger FL emission at longer wavelength. The evolution trend of the computed FL results shows remarkable accordance with the experimental data, providing connections between GN and EDFL both experimentally and theoretically.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multiplex metal-ion detection based on the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The multiple FL emissions of CD samples were employed in application of multiplex metal ion recognition. With the controllable synthetic strategy, the structure and FL performance can be well regulated for the construction of multiple sensing channels. After an elaborate screening process, three FL emissions were selected (CD1: λ em = 420 nm, λ ex = 315 nm; CD3: λ em = 413 nm, λ ex = 315 nm and λ em = 513 nm, λ ex = 390 nm), yielding a metal ion sensor based on CD probes. Firstly, the pH test window of the sensor was obtained by measuring FL performance under different pH environments (Fig. S10). The results indicate that the test window is between pH 8 and 11. The responsive behaviours of FL emissions to metal ions were measured by adding different kinds of metal ion solution with constant concentration into CD solution, respectively. All the responsive FL signals, enhanced or quenched, were recorded and processed by principal component analysis (PCA) and hierarchical cluster analysis (HCA) [41,42]. As a result, seventeen types of metal ions, including Hg2+, Ag+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Al3+, Co2+, Cr3+, Na+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Pb+, Mg2+, Mn2+ and Ce3+, can be detected and recognized by the sensor based on selected CD probes. All the responsive signals forming three sensing channels are shown in Figs. S11 and S12 and Table S6. Notably, the construction of two different sensing channels derived from CD3 was due to the different FL components on the CD sample, which acted like different chromophores. Moreover, the responsive mechanism was explored through FL lifetime measurement. As an example shown in Fig. S13, CD3 exhibited a significant FL lifetime decrease from 6.94 ns to 2.37 ns after adding Hg2+ ions, revealing a dynamic quenching based on electron transfer process [3,43]. The PCA result in Fig. 5 a shows that seventeen types of metal ions are separated exactly from each other, which can also be verified by HCA result in Fig. 5b, indicating completed and clear classification of all the seventeen types of metal ions. The results have demonstrated that the CDs prepared by our controllable synthetic method can be applied to construct a multi-analyte sensing platform, which can spread to other kinds of analytes. Furthermore, quantitative analysis against Fe3+ and Hg2+ ions based on blue emission of CD3 was performed, shown in Fig. S14 and Table S9, which can help the above statistical qualitative analysis to achieve more precise and detailed sensing results.

In this paper, we demonstrated a strategy to synthesize CDs with controllable EDFL. By employing nitrogen-contained precursors with different chemical reducibility in solvothermal reactions, a facile method to synthesize CDs doped with controllable amount of the GN was achieved. Detailed XPS analysis and other structure characterizations of four types of CDs revealed that the increasing GN in the CDs is the crucial structural motif causing the emergence of the EDFL. The GN content and consequence EDFL property can only emerge from strong reducibility nitrogen sources. Moreover, the role of GN in the emerging EDFL was in detail explained by theoretical calculations. As the GN content increases, the second FL emission at long wavelength revealed relative stronger intensity. Based on the different responsive behaviours of the specific FL emissions to metal ions, sensing channels were formed to achieve a CD-probes-based sensor. Accordingly, seventeen types of metal ions were detected and recognized through statistical analysis. In conclusion, we analysed the relationship between the chemical composition/structure and the FL emission of CDs, and intended to explore the structural motif responsible for the EDFL properties, which is meaningful for guiding synthesis of EDFL CDs and lay a foundation for their potential applications in multi-analyte detection in the future.

Changqing Ye: Investigation, Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing. Lei Xu: Investigation. Shuoran Chen: Investigation, Writing - original draft. Caihong Wang: Investigation. Meng Su: Software. Guoliang Dai: Software. Xiaomei Wang: Supervision. Fengyu Li: Supervision. Yanlin Song: Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The computing center for Fudan University is thanked for computer time. This work was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51873145, 51673143, 51603141), Excellent Youth Foundation of Jiangsu Scientific Committee (BK20170065), Natural Science Foundation of the Higher Education Institutions of Jiangsu Province (17KJA430016), Qing Lan Project, 5th 333 High-level Talents Training Project of Jiangsu Province (No. BRA2018340), Six Talent Summits Project of Jiangsu Province (No. XCL-79), Suzhou Key Laboratory for Nanophotonic and Nanoelectronic Materials and Its Devices (SZS201812).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphitic nitrogen triggers red fluorescence in carbon dots
